coredata.dc:title: Quick transformation of polymeric waste into high valuable N-self doped carbon quantum dot for detection of heavy metals from wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Bhakare, Madhuri A.
Bondarde, Mahesh P.
Lokhande, Kshama D.
Dhumal, Pratik S.
Some, Surajit
coredata.dc:description: Preparing carbon dots (CDs) from waste materials is essential in the current scenario. Herein, we converted non-disposable environmental pollutants into multifaceted, robust, and cost-effective sensing nitrogen-doped fluorescent CDs (N-CQDs) to quickly detect analytes in a wide range of samples. Herein, we studied the simple and efficient way for large-scale synthesis of the N-CQDs from waste melamine sponge (WMS) and lemon juice (LJ) by the treatment of concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4) within 10 min and the yield of 60.9 wt% obtained, much higher than those from traditional processes. As-prepared N-CQDs showed bright blue fluorescence and were significantly quenched with Hg2+ (turn off) completely regained by S2− (turn on) due to higher affinity S2− towards Hg2+. The detection limit was 1.04 µM for Hg2+ and 1.62 µM for S2−. This study provides a new perspective for developing N-CQDs and selective sensing Hg2+ and S2− from wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Heteroatom doping
Fluorescence quenching
Mercury ion
Sulfide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N doped carbon dots
Fluorescence turns off detection of Hg2+
Selective fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs with Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ and S2− in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Fourier transform infrared spectrum of N-CQDS. b) Thermogravimetric curve of N-CQDS.
The XPS a) survey scan and high resolution b) C 1s spectra, c) O1s spectra, and d) N1s spectra of as-prepared N-CQDS.

a) UV Vis absorbance and emission of the spectrum of N-CQDs, b) FL emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 390 nm, c) Selectivity of N-CQDS in the presence of various metal ions, d) Emission spectra of aqueous N-CQDS at different pH conditions.
a) FL emission spectra of N-CQDS in the presence of various concentrations of Hg2+. b) Stern Volmer plots for the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by Hg2+ ions in aqueous solutions.

a) FL emission spectra of N-CQDs-Hg2+ (50 ppm) in the presence of various concentrations of S2− (0–200 ppm), b) The linear relationship between relative FL intensity and concentration of S2−, c) Fluorescent response of the N-CQDs-Hg2+ towards anions. d) Schematic of fluorescence on-off by mercury and sulfide.
Schematic design of a) N-CQDs synthesis process and b) fluorescent quenching mechanism of the N-CQDS in the existence of the metal ions such as Hg2+.
Schematic diagram of synthesis of N-CQDs with detection of Hg2+ and S2− using turn-off on fluorescence probe N-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Quick transformation of polymeric waste into high valuable N-self doped carbon quantum dot for detection of heavy metals from wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Speciality Chemicals Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, Mumbai 400019, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Speciality Chemicals Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, Mumbai 400019, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Speciality Chemicals Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, Matunga, Mumbai 400019, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparing carbon dots (CDs) from waste materials is essential in the current scenario. Herein, we converted non-disposable environmental pollutants into multifaceted, robust, and cost-effective sensing nitrogen-doped fluorescent CDs (N-CQDs) to quickly detect analytes in a wide range of samples. Herein, we studied the simple and efficient way for large-scale synthesis of the N-CQDs from waste melamine sponge (WMS) and lemon juice (LJ) by the treatment of concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4) within 10 min and the yield of 60.9 wt% obtained, much higher than those from traditional processes. As-prepared N-CQDs showed bright blue fluorescence and were significantly quenched with Hg2+ (turn off) completely regained by S2− (turn on) due to higher affinity S2− towards Hg2+. The detection limit was 1.04 µM for Hg2+ and 1.62 µM for S2−. This study provides a new perspective for developing N-CQDs and selective sensing Hg2+ and S2− from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of the world economy, water contamination is one of the focus of universal problems as lots of industrial waste containing heavy metal ions have been discharged into the water bodies. Due to increased agricultural and industrial activities, incorrect metal ion discharge, and domestic sewage from effluents, the pollution of heavy metals such as mercury (Hg2+), arsenic (As2+), cadmium (Cd2+), and lead (Pb2+) has utmost serious issues by the flora and fauna (Squadron et al., 2015). Therefore, using easy and affordable methods to detect metal ions in various media for assessing ecological safety and human well-being. Hg2+ is one of the most dangerous metal ions for humans because of the potent interactions it has with protein sulfhydryl groups in a variety of organs, such as the brain, immune system, central nervous system, and kidneys (Ha et al., 2017; MacLean et al., 2013). Also, sulfide (S2−) is a toxic anion irritating mucous membranes. Moreover, in higher concentrations of S2−, may cause respiratory paralysis and unconsciousness (Zeng et al., 2022) Thus, from the viewpoint of human health and environmental protection concerns, efficient analytical techniques are needed to detect Hg2+. To date, several techniques are available including inductively-coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) biosensor, (Mandal et al., 2013) fluorescent assays (Chai et al., 2010) and atomic absorption spectroscopy for detection of Hg2+ and S2−. But many of these techniques have limitations due to their cost, sophisticated instruments and lengthy reaction time. Due to its quick analysis and great sensitivity, fluorescence assay may be the best option out of these for detecting heavy metal ions (Chai et al., 2010).

At Claremonton University, researchers purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2006 made the following discovery and gave it the name: Atomic carbon dots (CDs) (Xu et al., 2004). The photoluminescent carbon dot (CDs) as innovative, promising carbon nanomaterials below 10 nm size fascinated great attention owing to their commercial preparation, water solubility, higher optical properties, and non–toxicity. As mentioned above properties open up the entrance for a wide ranging applications (Miao et al., 2019), involving biomedicine (Hsu and Chang, 2012), bioimaging (Jiang et al., 2015), metal ion sensing, fluorescent probe (Zhang et al., 2016), catalysis, photovoltaic devices and fluorescent paint (Young Park et al., 2015). CQDs were prepared using the top-down and bottom-up techniques. Arc discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation all are including top-down method. Wherever this method “breaks off” the large carbon molecule to the formation of CQDs. Whereas, bottom up techniques involved plasma, ultrasonic, microwave, template and hydrothermal methods for synthesis of CQDs (Baker and Baker, 2010). But several preparation techniques include costly or used to poisonous starting precursors, extraordinary temperature and lengthy reaction time. Also, the product yields of these synthesized CQDs are very low (Liu et al., 2017). Hence, researchers have used two types of techniques to improve product yields and fluorescence properties of CQDs. The first technique is heteroatom doping into the CQDs, which can enhance the fluorescence properties and quantum yield of CQDs (Miao et al., 2020). Another technique is utilizing aromatic polymers as raw materials because they conjugate between fluorophore groups and unsaturated moieties. They can increase electron densities on the surface of CQDs and ultimately lead to effective fluorescence emission (Zhou et al., 2017; Pan et al., 2021). Doping heteroatoms such as sulfur (S), nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and boron (B) are one of the most appropriate strategies for having to introduce more defects, changing the electron configuration, CQDs density and optical properties can be tailored (Lu et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2022; Kong et al., 2016). Up to date, researchers have used N dopant sources such as L-asparagine, ammonia, ethylenediamine, and hydrazine hydrate (Wang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020). Traditional polymer disposal methods, such as incineration and landfill, pollute the atmosphere and soil (Wang et al., 2019). Hence, we designed to convert WMS into most valuable product. We have to reuse WMS, which contains high amount of N-CQDs green preparation CQDs mostly centre attention on the choice of green natural resources such as LJ. In comparison with another toxic organic compounds like aromatic hydrocarbon molecules, LJ is most favourable for the preparation of CQDs due to its renewability, cost-effectiveness, natural abundance, and eco-friendly (Monte-Filho et al., 2019). Due to the renewability of the raw precursors and the cost-effective fabrication of CDs on large scale production possible, whichever is of enormous importance as industrial manufacture and application of CQDs.

Herein, we report one pot synthesis of N-CQDs from waste melamine sponge (WMS) and LJ as raw materials and concentrated H2SO4. LJ contains high concentration of citric acid, and ascorbic acid used as carbon, whereas the carbon also nitrogen sources were provided by WMS. Synthesized N-CQDs displayed decent water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and outstanding photoluminescence properties. The present study delivers cost-effective, sustainable precursors for the fabrication of N-CQDs and gives WMS as value-added products. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), is used to study the as-synthesized N-CQDs.

Concentrated Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) was purchased from oxford and Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)2) was received from sigma Aldrich. The waste melamine sponge (WMS) and lemon were purchased from local market in Mumbai. HgCl2, BaCl2, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, AlCl3, FeSO4, Pb (NO3)2, MgCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, and NiCl2, were purchased from Oxford, Mumbai. All chemicals were analytical grade and used them without further purifications. All solutions were prepared in deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs were prepared using H2SO4 within 10 min in a one-pot synthesis. Briefly, WMS was cut into small pieces, washed with acetone, then deionized water, and dried in an oven. Next, 5 g of dried WMS was immersed in 15 mL of LJ for 1 h, followed by drying at 90 °C for 2 h. Then, 7 mL of concentrated H2SO4 was added to this dried LJ containing WMS for 10 min. Next, water was added to quench the reaction. After cooling the reaction mass, a solid Ca (OH) 2 powder was added until pH 7, and calcium sulfate (CaSO4) was precipitated. Then, this mixture was filtrated using 0.22 µm filter paper, and the supernatant liquid was rota-evaporated to obtain a solid powder. The resulting solid powder was used for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turns off detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The Hg2+ detection study was carried out in aqueous solutions. Freshly prepared Hg2+ in various concentrations, such as 1–200 ppm. Metal ion solutions of different concentrations were combined separately with 1 mg/mL of N-CQDs aqueous dispersion at a volume ratio of 1:1 to test the sensitivity of the N-CQDs solution as produced to Hg2+. Afterward sonicated at room temperature for 2 min, the fluorescence (FL) intensity in the presence (F) and absence (F0) of Hg2+ was measured through the excitation wavelength at 370 nm. The relative fluorescence ratio (F/F0) and fluorescence quenching (F0/F) were used for calibration. After being sonicated at room temperature for 2 min, the fluorescence (FL) intensity was determined using a 370 nm excitation wavelength to distinguish between the presence (F) and absence (F0) of Hg2+. For calibration, the ratio fluorescence quenching (F0/F) and relative fluorescence (F/F0) were utilized. The sensing study was done three times.

As S2− can recover the quenched fluorescence of N-CQDs by Hg2+, fluorescence turn-off-on mode was applied to detect S2−. Briefly, N-CQDs (1 mg/ mL) were mixed with 50 ppm (Hg2+) and sonicated for 2 min. Then, a newly prepared solution of S2− was added at different concentrations (0–200 ppm). After that, this solution was further sonicated for 2 min at room temperature. The photoluminescence intensity was recorded at 370 excitation wavelength. The relative fluorescence recovery ratio (F0/F) was used to evaluate the fluorescence recovery via S2−, where F0 and F represent the FL intensity in the absence and presence of S2−, respectively.

Detection of pH is extremely important since it plays a significant role in various systems, especially in nanomedicine, chemical sensing, and bioimaging. The pH detecting ability of the aqueous solution of the N-CQDs was studied under 370 nm excitation at room temperature for a wide range from pH 1–12, which were attuned via adding the 1 M aqueous solution of HCl and NaOH.

To investigate the practicality of the system, the N-CQDs sensing system was applied to detect Hg2+ and S2− in tap water and river water samples. The tap water is collected from our lab without any pre-treatment. The river water sample was obtained from the Mithi River (Mumbai, India). The collected water was first filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then centrifuged for 15 min. These water samples were then mixed with Hg2+ and S2− solutions to obtain different concentrations (45 μM, 55 μM, and 85 μM), following which the N-CQD and N-CQD-Hg2+ were added to these solutions. The fluorescence intensity quantifies the contents of Hg2+ and S2− in the solutions. All experiments were performed three times.

The morphology and shape of as-prepared N-CQDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Brukar alfa II) was used to determine the functional groups present in N-CQDs. Jasco V 750 spectrometer was used to obtain UV/vis spectra of the as-prepared N-CQDs. Fluorescence samples were recorded using a Jasco FP 8200 Spectrophotometer. The thermal stability of N-CQDs was analyzed by the STA7300 thermal analysis system.

The FTIR spectra of N-CQDS are shown in (Fig. 1a). The broad peak at 3398 cm−1 for O-H and N-H (Donald et al., 1996). The peak at 2960 cm−1 indicated the presence of C-H stretching vibrations. The characteristics peak at 1625 cm−1 for CC bonding. Another peak at 1440 cm−1, 1014 was assigned for C-N stretching and C-O-C stretching (Donald et al., 1996). FTIR spectra show CQD successfully doping nitrogen functionalities. Thermogravimetry (TG) and its derivative (DTG) are analytical methods used for determining the thermal stability and the proportion of volatile components by observing the weight change that occurs with sample heating as shown in Fig. 1b and S3. The N-CQDSs decomposed in three steps. The first mass loss at about 150 °C for water molecules (Pires et al., 2015). The maximum decomposition occurs between 200 °C to 300 °C for depolymerization, decarbonylation, and loss of surface hydroxylated groups. Finally, the mass loss is up to 600 °C for graphitic carbons (Pires et al., 2015). The height of the DTG peak shows the rate of mass decomposition kinetics. According to the Derivative Thermogravimetric (DTG) curve (Fig. S3), The N-CQD showed a narrow peak and degradation between 130 °C to 289 °C due to the removal of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and nitrogen-containing groups (Tolou-Shikhzadeh-Yazdi et al., 2023). DTG peaks give information about the ongoing process of degradation. The decomposition peak gained a second rate of mass loss at 635 °C likely due to the elimination of pyridine also pyrrolidine groups (Şenel et al., 2019).

The chemical modification of N-CQDs was analyzed by using the XPS technique. Fig. 2 discloses that the as-prepared N-CQDs contains carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Two prominent structures of the C1s and O1s peaks attended by the one small peaks of N1s have been observed in the survey spectrum demonstrated in Fig. 2a, indicating that the surface of N-CQDs is composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. We further characterized the high resolution of C1s, O1s, and N1s. In Fig. 2b, the high-resolution C1s spectrum exhibited four peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.6 eV, 288.1 eV, and 288.5 eV for CC/C-C, C-O/C-N, CO, and CO (OH) respectively. The peak is present at 284.4 eV for CC/C-C for graphitic or aliphatic structures (Yang et al., 2016). The high-resolution O1s spectra are shown in Fig. 2c, mainly two peaks at 532.2 eV and 533.1 eV, which are assigned to CO and C-O (Khose et al., 2022). The presence of oxygen functionality in the N-CQDs helps to improve water solubility as well as another polar medium. The N1s spectra showed two peaks for C-N and N-H groups at 398.9 eV and 401.8 eV. All these groups enhance the stability and hydrophilicity of N-CQDs (Iqbal et al., 2018).

The optical properties of as-prepared N-CQDs were studied using UV Vis and PL spectroscopy. The UV–visible absorption spectra of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 a. The prominent absorption peak, which corresponds to the π–π* transition, is located at 238 nm (Qu et al., 2013). The FL spectra of corresponding N-CQDs solutions at various excitation wavelengths between 280 and 390 nm are shown in Fig. 3b. The surface state of functional oxygenic groups (such as OH and CO) causes the FL intensity and maximum emission wavelengths to be strongly correlated with the excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 3b, the maximum excitation at 370 gives the maximum emission at 480 nm (Fig. 3a). It is worth observing that the maximum FL emission of N-CQDs remains unchanged after the excitation wavelength changes Additionally, the N-CQDs exhibits outstanding photoluminescence properties for optical sensing when exposed to UV light at 370 nm (Fig. S2), which causes it to generate strong intense blue fluorescence. As seen in Fig. 3b, an exceptional blue PL at UV light was seen in the case of N-CQDs. The highest emission wavelength of N-CQDs remains at 480 nm despite an increase in excitation wavelength from 280 to 360 nm, which may result from a novel surface state produced by the doped nitrogen atoms. The highest emission wavelength slightly shifts to the red from 380 to 390 nm when the excitation wavelength rises further to 380 nm. Table S1 compares the synthesis of CQD from different methods and their Hg2+ detection of LOD values. As per the reported literature, the B-CQD synthesis hydrothermal treatment for 12 h at 180 °C temperature and LOD values towards Hg2+ was 2.6 µM. PNBS-CQDs-Fe2+ was prepared by hydrothermal treatment for 5 h at 200 °C temperature and LOD values for Hg2+ were 2.6 µM. AACD was also prepared from thermolysis for 3 h at 160 °C and a LOD value of 3.7 µM for Hg2+. However, this method is expensive, requires sophisticated instruments, and is time-consuming. As prepared, N-CQDs are quickly synthesized without using sophisticated instruments and mass scale production within 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs with Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The variation in the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/Hg2+ was scrutinized by observing the intensity difference by adding various metal ions from 1 ppm, 10 ppm, 50 ppm, 100 ppm, 120 ppm, and 150 ppm to 200 ppm. For sensing applications, specificity, and selectivity are crucial factors. In this study, 200 ppm of different metal ions including Li+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Al3+, and Fe2+, as well as Ag+ as the interfering ions were added to N-CQDs to examine its selectivity. No noticeable reduction in emission intensity is seen with the addition of interfering metal ions, as illustrated in Fig. 3c. On the other hand, when Hg2+ is added to detecting solutions, the fluorescence intensity dramatically reduces. Numerous studies have revealed that the N-CQDs is capable of reliably detecting Hg2+ (Zhang et al., 2016; Iqbal et al., 2018). Owing to the good soft acid-soft base binding affinity with N atoms as in graphitic carbon structure with Hg2+, the N-CQDs in this work exhibit better selectivity for Hg2+ sensing than that of Pb2+. Hence, selective fluorescence response of N-CQDs with Hg2+ could be achieved. It is also significant that N-CQDs prepared by waste melamine sponge and citric acid can effectively detect Hg2+ from wastewater. The pH level of the solution is partially correlated with the PL characteristics of N-CQDs in aqueous solutions. Additionally, in the presence of 200 ppm Hg2+, the impact of pH on the effectiveness of Hg2+ sensing was further examined. To further understand the efficacy of the fluorescent N-CQDs, we studied their fluorescence intensity under various pH conditions. According to Fig. 3d, the solution of the N-CQDs, excited at 370 nm, showed a variation in its fluorescence intensity dependent on different pH conditions. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs increases with higher pH values at 9.5 and decreases with lower pH values up to 3.4, which may be attributed to protonation and de-protonation of the carboxyl groups on the N-CQDs surface. It should also be mentioned that the produced N-CQDs maintained their fluorescence intensity, decreasing even at highly acidic pH 3.4 conditions, and enhanced fluorescence intensity at alkaline pH 9.5 (Donald et al., 1996; Pyne et al., 2019). To achieve the highest sensitivity, the detection conditions, including pH and development time, were optimized. As shown in Fig. S4, the highest Fluorescence quenching exists at pH 9.5. A low pH value (pH 3.4 and 6) might weaken the interaction between N-CQDs and metal ions (Hg2+) through the protonation of N atoms on CQDs whereas a high pH value (pH 9.5) shows stronger interaction between N-CQDs and Hg2+ (Donald et al., 1996; Pyne et al., 2019).

The sensitivity of N-CQDS to Hg2+ detection was next evaluated following the successful characterizations by comparing the variation in fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of Hg2+. Fig. 4 a displays the difference in fluorescence intensity of N-CQDS after the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ ranging from 0 to 200 ppm. N-CQDs fluorescence intensity decreases as Hg2+ concentration rises, and at 200 ppm Hg2+, 92% of the initial fluorescence intensity is quenched. In order to understand the quenching process of the system, a Stern–Volmer plot was built to study the processes for interaction between N-CQDs and the Hg2+ ion. The fluorescence-quenching procedure is typically produced by the interaction of quenching chemicals with fluorescence molecules, which frequently involves either static or dynamic quenching. The fluorescence quenching data of N-CQDs towards Hg2+ were analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation to further explore the effect of Hg2+ fluorescence quenching on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. The fluorescence quenching can be described by Stern Volmer equation (1).
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
C
Where F0 and F are the fluorescent intensities of N-CQDs at 370 nm in the absence and presence of a quencher (in this study, Hg2+ is the quencher), Ksv is the Stern Volmer constant of the quencher, and C is the Hg2+ concentration. The Stern−Volmer plot shown in Fig. 4b fits a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.99006) over the 1.–50 μM concentration range.

The possible PL quenching mechanism can be interpreted by the fluorescence quenching spectra of the Stern–Volmer curves of N-CQDs with Hg2+ at different temperatures (308 K, 313 K) as shown in (Fig. S5a). Fig. S5a shows that the Stern-Volmer constant Ksv decreases with increasing temperature and the Stern-Volmer plots are linear, indicating a static quenching interaction between Hg2+ and N-CQDs (Xu et al., 2016).

Fluorescence intensity data were used to determine the binding constant (K) and the number of binding sites (n). The modified Stern-Volmer equation determines the equilibrium between free and bound molecules for the static quenching process when small molecules bind independently to a series of equivalent sites of the N-CQD (each binding site has the same capacity for binding a quencher).
(2)
log
F 0
- F
F
= l o g
K s
+ n log
Q
where F0 and F are corrected fluorescence intensities, in the absence and in the presence of a quencher, respectively; Ks is the binding constant, and n is the number of binding sites. Linear dependence log (F0 − F)/F = (log [Q]), shown in Fig. S5b, is used to obtain apparent binding constant values and the number of binding sites. The binding constant Ks and n has been found to be directly proportional to temperature (Vaishanav et al., 2021). Results are shown in Table S2 the number of binding sites (n) is approximately equal to 1 at 308 K and 313 K studied. This indicates that there is one binding site between N-CQDs and Hg2+. And binding constant is 6.4 ×104 at 313 K indicating the strongest ability to bind with Hg2+. Overall, these results confirm that the N-CQDs can be used as a nanoprobe for Hg2+ detection. Using the formula 3/ δ, the LOD for Hg2+ is calculated to be 1.04 µM, where is the slope of the linear calibration plot and is the standard deviation of the lowest fluorescence signal (Dutta Chowdhury and Doong, 2016). Interestingly, the presence of heteroatoms would increase the surface flaws of CQDs, leading to an increase in sp2 conjugated carbon clusters that would increase fluorescence intensity (Wu and Tong, 2019). They are able to provide a wide and consistent quantitative range for sensing, which raises the sensitivity of N-CQDs for detecting Hg2+. Through extremely small LOD and a wider dynamic range, nitrogen doping in this study improved the detection sensitivity of Hg2+.

The N-CQDs quenched fluorescence Hg2+ can be recovered by the addition of S2− due to their strong coordination and Hg2+ high affinity towards S2− (Zhang et al., 2016). Because of the fluorescence recovery phenomena, the N-CQDs-Hg2+ system can be used to detect S2− selectively. The fluorescence recovery response of the 50 ppm N-CQDS-Hg2+ system was investigated under optimal conditions at different S2− concentrations (0 to 200 ppm) shown in Fig. 5 a. As the S2− concentration increases, the N-CQDs fluorescence intensity gradually returns. Fig. 5b displays that the enhancement of fluorescent intensity has good linearity with increasing concentrations of sulfides (R2 = 0. 9857). The LOD is discovered to be 1.62 µM toward S2−, and a linear correlation is formed between the N-CQDs-Hg2+ fluorescence intensity and the concentration of S2− ranging from 0 to 200 ppm (Fig. 5b). To examine the selectivity of the proposed approach for sulfide detection possible coexisting anions, CH3COO−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, and Br−. Fig. 5c shows N-CQDs-Hg2+ sensing platform has better selectivity for sulfide detection. Schematic representations for fluorescence are on-off have been given in Fig. 5d.

The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibit high fluorescent properties. The plausible mechanism of doping N in CQD is a new surface state of CQDs. Addition of Hg2+ in this solution, the FL intensity decreases expressively. Fig. 6 shows a possible elucidation for the sensitive detection of Hg2+ via fluorescent quenching with N-CQDs/Hg2+. During illumination, the metal ion was attached to the surface of CQDs via functional groups through non-covalent bonds or electrostatic attraction. When specific metal ions are recognized, the distance between N-CQDs and metal ions decreases, resulting in a stronger interaction between N-CQDs and Hg2+, which significantly encourages charge transfer between N-CQDs/Hg2+ and thus decreases N-CQDs fluorescence (Zhang et al., 2016). This quenching effect is caused by the inherent emission effect of excited state electron transfer caused by photon absorption between the fluorophore and the metal ions, which encourages electron-hole pair recombination with the resulting non-radiative process, as shown in Fig. 6.

The PL quenching of N-CQDs is also affected by the concentration of the target analyte, allowing for the detection of toxic metal ions. It has already been mentioned that until they are close to each other, fluorescence quenching of the fluorophore with metal ions is feasible in both the bound states (Liu et al., 2014). Hg2+ quenches the fluorescence of N-CQDs with a high efficiency, which increases the sensitivity of charge transfer-based sensing systems. Due to a reduced emission gap, a lower distance between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) increases the likelihood of achieving a nonradiative transition. Additionally, structural relaxation during photoexcitation causes the emission gap to shrink relative to the absorption gap (Chen et al., 2018). The N-CQD-Hg2+ system's possible quenching mechanism is that the N-CQDs surface oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl or carboxyl groups, can coordinate with the Hg2+ ions to form complexes and cause an electron transfer from the N-CQDs to the Hg2+ ions. N is introduced into CQDs, which encourages coordination interactions between Hg2+ and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. Therefore, noticeable FL quenching of the N-CQD-Hg2+ system can result from enhanced interactions and fast electron-transfer processes. Due to sulfide's significant affinity for Hg2+ upon addition to the N-CQDs-Hg2+ system and the subsequent production of the Hg-S bond, Hg2+ is removed from the surface of the N-CQDs, allowing for the activation of their fluorescence. In this study, the relative quantum of fluorescence (QYF) yield of as prepared aqueous N-CQDs was 33.2 %. The quinine sulphate (QYF = 0.54 in water with 0.5 M H2SO4) was used as a standard reference to comparably quantify the QYF of N-CQDs. The QYF was calculated according to the following equation (3) (Ju et al., 2014):
(3)
φ s = φ r
A s
A r
I s
I r
η
s
2
η
r
2
Where
φ
represents quantum yield,
η
is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the absorbance, and I is the integral area of the emission intensity. The subscript r and s refer to a reference standard and s refer to a sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and S2− in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of fluorescence probes in real water samples determines their application prospect. To evaluate the applicability of this N-CQDs fluorescent probe in real water, the standard addition method was used to verify the accuracy of the proposed sensor (Wu and Tong, 2019; Wang et al., 2023). Table S3 shows that with the addition of Hg2+standard solutions at different concentrations, the measured spiked recoveries in tap water range from 99.65% to 101.87 %, and in river water range from 97.88 to 103.63 % indicating a good recovery rate of Hg2+ in real samples. Table S4 shows that with the addition of S2− standard solutions at different concentrations, the measured spiked recoveries in tap water range from 97.43 to 102.33.%, and in river water range from 99.33 to 104.54 % indicating the good recovery rates of S2− in real samples. Thus, the fluorescent probe can quantitatively measure Hg2+ and S2−. These results indicated the reliability of N-CQD and the potential for actual detection in real samples.

Herein, we studied a novel, facile and cost-effective method for synthesizing highly efficient N-CQDs from WMS and renewable source LJ by the treatment of concentrated H2SO4 for the detection with Hg2+ and S2−. Using waste MS will not only turn the waste into a more valuable product but also reduce the manufacturing cost for mass production of N-CQDs. The combination of these two components, which are WMS and LJ, brings both good photoluminescent properties and excellent selectivity, allowing the proposed biosensor to have extraordinary sensitivity and specificity to Hg2+ and S2− analysis against other analytes. Our approach shows a high production yield (60.9 %), which is higher than the reported hydrothermal and microwave methods. The XPS and FT-IR studies confirmed the successful doping of nitrogen into CQDs. The doping of nitrogen atoms improved the sensitivity and selectivity. Overall outcomes of this study designated the N-CQDs to be able to sensitively also selectively sense Hg2+ with LOD 1.04 µM and 1.62 µM for S2− in wastewaters, which can open an innovative platform for building a N-CQDs probe with robust and can reliably and quickly detect metal ions in biological and chemical fluid samples. In addition, prominent advantages with low-cost, sustainable, facile methods have excellent prospective in different industrial applications of N-CQDs, including Hg2+ and S2−.
CSIR fund (22/0748/17/14/EMR-II).
DST-FIST-2018 Grant Section Number (SR/FST/ET-I/2018/156(C)).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the CSIR (22/0748/17/14/EMR-II), COE-PI TEQIP III fund, DST FIST (SR/FST/ET-I/2018/156(C)), and SARTHI.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2023.119150.

ce:section.ce:para:
